sources.sourceName: Introduction to Sociology, OpenStax 3e (Pressbooks adaptation) - Appendix: Chapter Quiz Answers (odd-numbered)

meta.notes: Sociology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e Ch. 14 and Ch. 15 section-quiz pages; correct answers from the OpenStax-derived odd-numbered answer key (Pressbooks CC BY 4.0 adaptation). Live OpenStax page shows CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

hints.prompt: To say a society 'sanctions' certain relationships as family means society does what to them?
hints.rationale: To sanction here means to officially recognize or approve a relationship.

hints.prompt: One option limits family to 'the connection of bloodlines.' Why is that narrower than the sociological view?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because it excludes marriage and adoption, which sociologists also count

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because bloodlines are entirely irrelevant to family

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because it includes far too many people

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because it is identical to the sociological view

hints.rationale: Defining family by bloodlines alone leaves out marriage and adoption that the sociological definition includes.

hints.prompt: Which two options describe features found WITHIN a family rather than how society DEFINES who counts as family?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'the status roles that exist in a family structure' and 'how closely members adhere to social norms'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'how a society sanctions relationships through blood, marriage, or adoption' and 'the connection of bloodlines'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'the connection of bloodlines' and 'the status roles in a family structure'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'how closely members adhere to social norms' and 'how a society sanctions relationships'

hints.rationale: Internal roles and norm-adherence describe what happens inside families, not the basis on which society defines family.

questions.stem: Sociologists tend to define family in terms of

choices.label: A
choices.text: how a given society sanctions the relationships of people who are connected through blood, marriage, or adoption

choices.label: C
choices.text: the status roles that exist in a family structure

choices.label: D
choices.text: how closely members adhere to social norms

questions.explanation: Sociologists define family broadly in terms of how a society recognizes relationships among people connected by blood, marriage, or adoption, rather than by bloodline alone.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 14 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q1 = A.

hints.prompt: Marriage involving more than two partners at once is generally called what?
hints.rationale: Having multiple spouses at once is polygamy.

hints.prompt: Within multiple-spouse marriage, the case of ONE MAN having several wives is called what?
hints.rationale: One man with multiple wives is polygyny, the mirror image of the case in the stem.

hints.prompt: The stem describes a woman MARRIED to two men. Which two options can be eliminated, one because it means only one spouse and the other because it means an unmarried couple living together?
hints.rationale: Monogamy means a single spouse and cohabitation means living together unmarried, neither of which fits one woman married to two men.

questions.stem: A woman being married to two men would be an example of:
questions.explanation: Polyandry is the form of polygamy in which one woman has multiple husbands. (Polygyny is one man with multiple wives.)
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 14 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q3 = C.

hints.prompt: The 'family life cycle model' describes families as passing through what?
hints.rationale: The model describes families moving through a fixed sequence of stages.

hints.prompt: Two options offer OPPOSITE criticisms (too broad versus too narrow). Given the model imposes a fixed set of stages that may not fit diverse families, the fitting criticism is that it is too ______.
hints.rationale: The criticism is that the model is too narrow or rigid to fit the diversity of real families.

hints.prompt: Which two criticisms are the weakest because they dismiss the model as useless or unresearched, which does not fit a widely used model?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'It does not serve a practical purpose for studying family behavior' and 'It is not based on comprehensive research'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'It is too narrowly focused on a sequence of stages' and 'It is too broad'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'It is too broad and accounts for too many aspects of family' and 'It does not serve a practical purpose'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'It is not based on comprehensive research' and 'It is too narrowly focused on a sequence of stages'

hints.rationale: Claiming the model is useless or unresearched does not fit a widely used, studied model.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a criticism of the family life cycle model?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is too broad and accounts for too many aspects of family.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is too narrowly focused on a sequence of stages.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It does not serve a practical purpose for studying family behavior.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is not based on comprehensive research.

questions.explanation: A common criticism is that the family life cycle model is too narrowly focused on a fixed sequence of stages, which does not fit the diversity of real families.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 14 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q5 = B.

hints.prompt: The cited study compares outcomes for children with married parents to children in other household types. This is a comparison of children's advantages across what?
hints.rationale: The study compares children's advantages across different household structures.

hints.prompt: For 'all of the above' to be correct, the study would have had to compare married-parent households to divorced, single, AND grandparent households at once. Since the item points to one specific comparison, 'all of the above' is likely what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An overreach beyond the single comparison cited

hints.rationale: The item cites one specific comparison, so 'all of the above' overreaches.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be ruled out, one because it names a less commonly cited household type and the other because it overreaches by combining every option?

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'all of the above' and 'a single parent'

hints.rationale: The grandparent household is a less-cited comparison and 'all of the above' overreaches, so both are weak.

questions.stem: According to the study cited by the U.S. Census Bureau, children who live with married parents grow up with more advantages than children who live with:
questions.explanation: Per the source, the cited Census Bureau study found that children with married parents tend to grow up with more advantages than children living with a single parent.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 14 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q7 = B.

hints.prompt: This item asks for a PERCENTAGE of all U.S. households that are same-sex couple households. That share is best described as what?
hints.rationale: Same-sex couple households are a small minority of all households.

hints.prompt: If the figure were 30%, that would mean nearly one in three households. Is that plausible for same-sex couple households?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; that is far too high

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes, and it is an undercount

hints.rationale: About one in three households would be far too high for same-sex couple households.

hints.prompt: Same-sex couple households are a small percentage. Which two figures are clearly too high because they would mean well over one in ten households?
hints.rationale: 15% and 30% would each mean far more than one in ten households, which is implausibly high.

questions.stem: Same-sex couple households account for _____ percent of U.S. households.
questions.explanation: Per the source, same-sex couple households make up roughly 1 percent of U.S. households.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 14 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q9 = A.

hints.prompt: This item describes the direction of change in U.S. divorce rates. To describe a rate as 'declining' means it is doing what?
hints.rationale: Declining means the rate is going down over time.

hints.prompt: Two options ('all-time high' and 'steadily increasing') both suggest divorce is becoming MORE common. The source instead indicates divorce is becoming what?
hints.rationale: The source indicates divorce is becoming less common, ruling out the high or increasing options.

hints.prompt: The source says divorce is going down. Which two options can be eliminated because they indicate divorce rising or at a peak?
hints.rationale: An all-time high and a steady increase both indicate rising divorce, which contradicts a decline.

questions.explanation: Per the source, current U.S. divorce rates are steadily declining after peaking in earlier decades.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 14 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q11 = D.

hints.prompt: The idea here is that divorce can be beneficial for children when it removes them from what?
hints.rationale: Divorce can benefit children by removing them from ongoing conflict.

hints.prompt: If a household has LITTLE conflict, would divorce tend to benefit the children in the same way?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; the benefit comes specifically from ending high conflict

hints.rationale: The benefit is tied to ending high conflict, so a low-conflict home does not show the same benefit.

hints.prompt: The benefit depends on the level of CONFLICT in the home. Which two options describe household composition rather than a level of conflict?
hints.rationale: Stepfamily and multigenerational describe who is in the household, not its level of conflict.

questions.stem: In general, children in ______ households benefit from divorce.
questions.explanation: Children in high-conflict households tend to benefit from divorce, because the divorce removes them from ongoing exposure to conflict.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 14 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q13 = C.

hints.prompt: Child maltreatment includes several types. 'Neglect' specifically refers to what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Failure to provide for a child's basic needs

choices.label: D
choices.text: A specific type of head injury

hints.rationale: Neglect is the failure to meet a child's basic needs.

hints.prompt: Neglect differs from physical abuse in that neglect is an act of ______ rather than a harmful action.
hints.rationale: Neglect is failing to act, unlike the direct harmful actions of physical or verbal abuse.

hints.prompt: Which two options are the least likely to be the single MOST prevalent category, one being a very specific injury and the other a hard-to-document form?
hints.rationale: A narrow specific injury and a hard-to-document form are unlikely to be the most prevalent reported category.

questions.stem: Which type of child abuse is most prevalent in the United States?
questions.explanation: Neglect is the most prevalent form of child maltreatment reported in the United States.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 14 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 14 Q15 = B.

hints.prompt: A 'social institution' is best described as what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An established, organized system that meets society's needs and organizes behavior

hints.rationale: A social institution is an organized system that meets needs and organizes behavior.

hints.prompt: Which of the following is a real way religion functions like a social institution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It provides a complex, integrated set of norms

choices.label: B
choices.text: It has no rules at all

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is unrelated to society's values

hints.rationale: Providing an integrated set of norms is one hallmark of religion as a social institution.

hints.prompt: You have confirmed that religion provides a complex, integrated set of norms. Before choosing that single option, what should you verify?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Whether the other listed functions of religion are ALSO true

choices.label: B
choices.text: Whether it happens to be option A

choices.label: C
choices.text: Whether the option has the most words

choices.label: D
choices.text: Nothing; the first true function is always the full answer

hints.rationale: Since religion may perform several of these functions at once, check the other options before settling on one.

questions.stem: In what ways does religion serve the role of a social institution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Religions have a complex and integrated set of norms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Religious practices and beliefs are related to societal values.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Religions often meet several basic needs.

questions.explanation: Religion functions as a social institution in all of these ways: it has integrated norms, connects to societal values, and meets basic social needs.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 15 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q1 = D.

hints.prompt: Studying how religion affects an INDIVIDUAL's sense of support and well-being is analysis at which level?
hints.rationale: Focusing on an individual's experience is micro-level analysis.

hints.prompt: Which perspective is known for MACRO-level analysis of large structures, the opposite of a micro focus?
hints.rationale: Functionalism is a macro-level perspective, unlike a micro focus on individuals.

hints.prompt: The item wants a MICRO-level perspective. Which two options are macro-level perspectives focused on large-scale structures and inequality?
hints.rationale: Functionalism and conflict theory both analyze large-scale structures, not micro-level individual experience.

questions.stem: Which of the main theoretical perspectives would approach religion from the micro-level, studying how religion impacts an individual's sense of support and well-being?
questions.explanation: Symbolic interactionism works at the micro level, examining how religion shapes individuals' meanings, support, and well-being.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 15 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: A perspective that emphasizes how institutions help MAINTAIN INEQUALITY between groups focuses primarily on what?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Power differences and inequality between groups

hints.rationale: This focus is on power differences and inequality between groups.

hints.prompt: A perspective that instead stresses how religion contributes to social STABILITY and shared values would be which?
hints.rationale: Functionalism stresses stability and shared functions, unlike a focus on maintaining inequality.

hints.prompt: Which two options are the poorest fit for 'maintaining social inequalities,' one because it stresses stability and shared functions and the other because it studies micro-level symbols?
hints.rationale: Functionalism emphasizes stability and symbolic interactionism examines micro-level symbols, so neither centers on maintaining inequality.

questions.stem: Which sociological perspective most emphasizes the ways in which religion helps to maintain social inequalities within a society?
questions.explanation: Conflict theory emphasizes how religion can legitimize and maintain social inequalities (e.g., Marx's view of religion as the 'opium of the people').
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 15 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q5 = C.

hints.prompt: The 'Protestant work ethic' links religious values like hard work and thrift to success in which domain?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Economic life and the accumulation of wealth

hints.rationale: The Protestant work ethic ties religious values to economic life and wealth accumulation.

hints.prompt: Why is 'evolution and natural selection' a poor fit for the Protestant work ethic?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because it is a biological theory, not an economic system tied to religious values

choices.label: B
choices.text: Because evolution is fundamentally about economics

choices.label: C
choices.text: Because it is the same idea as thrift

hints.rationale: Evolution and natural selection is a biological theory, unrelated to the economic emphasis of the work ethic.

hints.prompt: The work ethic was analyzed for its link to an economic system. Which two options are unrelated to economics, one being biological and the other about intergroup bias?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'evolution and natural selection' and 'prejudice and discrimination'

hints.rationale: Evolution is biological and prejudice concerns intergroup bias, so neither is the economic system linked to the work ethic.

questions.stem: The Protestant work ethic was viewed in terms of its relationship to:
questions.explanation: Max Weber linked the Protestant work ethic to the rise and spirit of capitalism.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 15 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q7 = B.

hints.prompt: In the sociology of religion, a 'sect' is generally what kind of group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A smaller group that has broken away from a larger religious body

hints.rationale: A sect is typically a smaller breakaway group from a larger religious body.

hints.prompt: One option says a sect has 'grown so large that it needs new buildings and multiple leaders.' Does that describe a sect or its opposite?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The opposite; that describes a large established body, not a sect

hints.rationale: Growing large with multiple leaders describes an established church, not a small breakaway sect.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be ruled out, one because it describes a large established church and the other because it depends on that false statement being true?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'has generally grown so large that it needs new buildings and multiple leaders' and 'All of the above'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'often believes it must split from the larger group' and 'is another term for a cult'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'is another term for a cult' and 'has generally grown so large...'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'All of the above' and 'often believes it must split from the larger group'

hints.rationale: Growing into a large church is the opposite of a sect, and 'all of the above' depends on that false claim, so both fail.

choices.label: A
choices.text: has generally grown so large that it needs new buildings and multiple leaders

choices.label: B
choices.text: often believes it must split from the larger group to return to important fundamentals

choices.label: C
choices.text: is another term for a cult

questions.explanation: A sect is typically a smaller breakaway group that splits from a larger religious body to return to what it sees as essential fundamentals.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 15 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q9 = B.

hints.prompt: The item distinguishes groups that are 'mislabeled as cults' from other categories. A group wrongly called a cult is better described by sociologists as what?
hints.rationale: Sociologists often call such groups new religious movements rather than cults.

hints.prompt: One option lists Quakers and Pentecostals. Are these better described as controversial groups mislabeled as cults, or as long-established Christian denominations?
hints.rationale: Quakers and Pentecostals are established Christian denominations, not groups mislabeled as cults.

hints.prompt: Which two options list groups infamous for tragic, deadly episodes, and thus widely viewed as genuinely dangerous rather than merely mislabeled?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'the Peoples Temple and Heaven's Gate' and 'the Branch Davidians and the Manson Family'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'Scientology and the Hare Krishna' and 'Quakers and Pentecostals'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'Quakers and Pentecostals' and 'the Peoples Temple and Heaven's Gate'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'the Branch Davidians and the Manson Family' and 'Scientology and the Hare Krishna'

hints.rationale: Those four groups are associated with notorious deadly events, so they are viewed as genuinely dangerous rather than simply mislabeled.

questions.stem: Some controversial groups that may be mislabeled as cults include:

choices.label: B
choices.text: the Peoples Temple and Heaven's Gate

choices.label: C
choices.text: the Branch Davidians and the Manson Family

questions.explanation: Per the source, groups such as Scientology and the Hare Krishna are sometimes mislabeled as cults, when sociologists would more neutrally call them new religious movements.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 15 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: Judaism, Christianity, and Islam are often grouped as the 'Abrahamic' religions because they share what?
hints.rationale: The Abrahamic religions share common narratives, figures, and roots.

hints.prompt: One option claims 'Judaism has no sacred text.' Is that accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; Judaism has a sacred text, such as the Torah

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only Islam has a sacred text

hints.rationale: Judaism does have a sacred text, so the claim that it has none is false.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated, one because it wrongly claims Judaism has no sacred text and the other because it oddly claims the stories were removed from the Torah?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'oral traditions only because Judaism has no sacred text' and 'no longer part of the Torah'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'shared by Christianity and Islam' and 'referred to as the Apocrypha'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'referred to as the Apocrypha' and 'no longer part of the Torah'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'oral traditions only because Judaism has no sacred text' and 'shared by Christianity and Islam'

hints.rationale: Judaism does have a sacred text, and the stories were not removed from the Torah, so both claims are false.

questions.stem: Many stories in the sacred text of Judaism are:

choices.label: B
choices.text: oral traditions only because Judaism has no sacred text

choices.label: D
choices.text: no longer part of the Torah

questions.explanation: As Abrahamic religions, Judaism, Christianity, and Islam share many of the same narratives and figures, so many stories in Judaism's sacred text are shared by Christianity and Islam.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 15 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q13 = C.

hints.prompt: The item describes using a church specifically to fight social and political INJUSTICE. This is a use of religion for what purpose?
hints.rationale: It describes using religion to advance social and political justice.

hints.prompt: The 'Protestant work ethic' concerns religion's link to what, rather than fighting injustice?
hints.rationale: The Protestant work ethic concerns religious values and economic success, not fighting political injustice.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be eliminated, one because it is about religion and economic success and the other because it is a vague, non-technical phrase rather than an established concept?
hints.rationale: The Protestant work ethic concerns economics and 'justice work' is not an established term, so neither names the concept described.

questions.stem: Social scientists refer to the use of a church to combat social injustice in the political realm as:
questions.explanation: Liberation theology is the use of religious institutions and teachings to combat social and political injustice.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 15 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 15 Q15 = C.
